critique.summary: Reviewed README.md, operators.py, and registry.py. The package has multiple concrete contract and correctness defects: the operator catalogue contains a duplicate name, the registry silently drops one operator, unknown-name lookup substitutes an unrelated operator instead of failing, precedence is keyed incorrectly relative to its API, and the binary-operator predicate accepts unary operators.

findings.description: operators.py, module-level OPERATORS: the unary negation operator is constructed as Operator("minus", "_", 1, ...) instead of having its own unique name. This duplicates the existing binary "minus" operator name, so names are not unique and any name-based lookup cannot distinguish binary subtraction from unary negation.

registry.py, module-level REGISTRY construction: REGISTRY = {op.name: op for op in OPERATORS} silently overwrites one operator when names collide. With the current OPERATORS list, the second "minus" replaces the first, so len(REGISTRY) is smaller than len(OPERATORS) and the binary minus operator is dropped from the registry entirely.

registry.py, lookup(): it returns REGISTRY.get(name, IDENTITY), so an unknown operator name returns OPERATORS[0] (the add operator) instead of raising KeyError. This violates the documented contract and silently substitutes a different operator for invalid input.

registry.py, precedence(): the function is documented to accept an operator symbol, but PRECEDENCE is keyed by names ("add", "minus", "times", "divide", "neg"). As a result, valid symbol queries like precedence("+") or precedence("*") return 0 instead of their actual precedence.

registry.py, is_binary(): it returns op.arity >= 1, which classifies any unary operator as binary. For example, the unary operator with arity 1 will return True even though the contract says is_binary(op) is True only for arity-2 operators.
